request.question: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?

title: Puttanna Kanagal Award
Kanagal, Mysore
Masanada Hoovu
Vijaya Bhaskar
Kappu Bilupu
P. H. Vishwanath
Puttanna Kanagal
Shivaram
Runamukthalu
John Cassavetes
sentences: The following is the list of Award winners for the Karnataka State Puttanna Kanagal award.
Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors.
In his memory and honor, this award is presented to the directors every year during the Karnataka State Awards function.
Kanagal is a village in Mysore district, Karnataka.
It is in periyapatna taluk 30 km from Taluk place.
It is very close to Coorg and Hassan district.
It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.
It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.
Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.
Masanada Hoovu (Kannada: ಮಸಣದ ಹೂವು ) ("Graveyard Flower") is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal, based on T. R. Subba Rao's novel of the same name.
It stars Jayanthi, Ambarish and Aparna in lead roles.
It was Puttanna Kanagal's last as a director.
His friend, director K. S. L. Swamy, completed the film.
"Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.
Vijaya Bhaskar (; 1924–2002) was an Indian music director and composer who composed music for several mainstream and experimental feature films in the Kannada film industry.
Scoring music for over 600 feature films, Bhaskar worked in Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, Marathi, Tulu and Konkani language films as well.
He was a part of all of popular Kannada film director KSL Swamy's (Ravi) movies and was also known for his association with acclaimed directors Puttanna Kanagal and Adoor Gopalakrishnan.
Kappu Bilupu (Kannada: ಕಪ್ಪು ಬಿಳುಪು, which means Black and White ) is a 1969 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal.
Based on a novel of the same name by Aryambha Pattabhi, this movie revolves around the contrasting behaviors of an identical, yet distinct pair of twins.
The film starred Kalpana in dual roles.
Puttanna himself remade the movie in Tamil as "Irulum Oliyum" and in Telugu as "Iddaru Ammayilu".
P. H. Vishwanath (Kannada: ಪಿ.
ಎಚ್.
ವಿಶ್ವನಾಥ್ ) is an Indian film director, writer and a producer who works in Kannada cinema.
Apart from films, Vishwanath has directed several documentaries and tele-series.
He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release.
Following this, he assisted for many films directed by Kanagal, Joe Simon, Hunsur Krishnamurthy and T. S. Nagabharana.

Puttanna Kanagal (born Shubraveshti Ramaswamiah Seetharama Sharma, 1 December 1933 – 5 June 1985), fondly called the 'Chitra Bramha' (God of Films, Kannada) was an Indian filmmaker and is considered as one of Kannada's cinema's greatest filmmakers.
S. Shivaram (born 1938), popularly known simply as Shivaram or Shivaramanna, is an Indian actor, producer and director whose Kannada cinema career has spanned six decades.
He has played roles including lead hero performances, character roles, comedic roles, as well as supporting parts.
He teamed up with his elder brother, S. Ramanathan and produced several films under the name Rashi Brothers apart from directing "Hrudaya Sangama" in 1972.
The brothers together produced few Bollywood films as well.
As an actor, Shivaram has the distinction of working with many stalwart directors out of which his association with Puttanna Kanagal is much remembered.
Runamukthalu (Kannada: ಋಣ ಮುಕ್ತಳು ) is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed and co-produced by Puttanna Kanagal starring Bharathi, Padmavasanthi and Ramakrishna.
The film is based on the novel "Runa" written by Anupama Niranjana.
The film was produced under the banner "Kanagal Creations".
John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.

Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.
He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).
He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.
His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.question: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?

He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Rosemary's Baby (film) | Rosemary's Baby is a 1968 American psychological horror film written and directed by Roman Polanski, based on the bestselling 1967 novel of the same name by Ira Levin.  The cast features Mia Farrow, John Cassavetes, Ruth Gordon, Sidney Blackmer, Maurice Evans, Ralph Bellamy, Angela Dorian, Clay Tanner, and, in his feature film debut, Charles Grodin.  The film chronicles the story of a pregnant woman who suspects that an evil cult wants to take her baby for use in their rituals.

Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors. He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release. It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.  It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.  Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.

It stars Jayanthi, Ambarish and Aparna in lead roles.  It was Puttanna Kanagal's last as a director.  His friend, director K. S. L. Swamy, completed the film.  "Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.

response.hop2_query: John Cassavetes "Rosemary's Baby" supporting passage actor in Hollywood films
hop2_docs: John Cassavetes | John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.  Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.  He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Rosemary's Baby (film) | Rosemary's Baby is a 1968 American psychological horror film written and directed by Roman Polanski, based on the bestselling 1967 novel of the same name by Ira Levin.  The cast features Mia Farrow, John Cassavetes, Ruth Gordon, Sidney Blackmer, Maurice Evans, Ralph Bellamy, Angela Dorian, Clay Tanner, and, in his feature film debut, Charles Grodin.  The film chronicles the story of a pregnant woman who suspects that an evil cult wants to take her baby for use in their rituals.

Z Channel: A Magnificent Obsession | Z Channel: A Magnificent Obsession is a 2004 documentary film about Los Angeles pay cable channel Z Channel which was directed by Xan Cassavetes, daughter of Hollywood director and actor John Cassavetes.  It was screened out of competition at the 2004 Cannes Film Festival.

Independent Spirit John Cassavetes Award | The Independent Spirit John Cassavetes Award is presented to the creative team of a film budgeted at less than $500,000 by the Film Independent, a non-profit organization dedicated to independent film and independent filmmakers.  It is named after actor/screenwriter/director John Cassavetes, a pioneer of American independent film.

Katherine Cassavetes | Katherine Cassavetes (née Demetre; June 24, 1906 – March 29, 1983) was a Greek American actress.  She was the mother of actor-director John Cassavetes and mother-in-law of actress Gena Rowlands.  Her grandchildren are actor-directors Nick Cassavetes, Zoe Cassavetes, and Alexandra Cassavetes.  She appeared in four films, three of which were written and directed by her son, including "Minnie and Moskowitz" (1971).

Alexandra Cassavetes | Alexandra "Xan" Cassavetes (born September 21, 1965) is an American actress and director.  She is the daughter of Greek-American actor-director John Cassavetes and actress Gena Rowlands.  She is the granddaughter of actress Katherine Cassavetes.  She is the sister of actor-director Nick Cassavetes and actor-screenwriter-director Zoe Cassavetes.

Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby | Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby (also known as Rosemary's Baby II) is a 1976 American made-for-television horror film and a sequel to Roman Polanski's 1968 film "Rosemary's Baby" starring Stephen McHattie, Patty Duke, George Maharis, Ruth Gordon and Ray Milland.  The film premiered as the "ABC Friday Night Movie" on October 29, 1976.
hop2_titles: Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby
